items.title: CPDP 2026: The Role of Operating Systems in Trusted AI
items.description: Panel moderation at CPDP 2026 examining DMA Article 6(7) interoperability obligations, the tension between forced OS openness and GDPR-level privacy protections, hardware and software access risks, and whether a single interoperability standard can work across Apple, Google, and other platforms.

rssMetadata.excerpt: Panel moderation at CPDP 2026 on DMA Article 6(7) interoperability and OS-level privacy, examining the tension between forced openness and GDPR protections across Apple, Google, and other platforms.

items.title: Expert Workshop: Web Standards for Data Protection in the EU
items.description: Invited participation in the RegTech4AI expert workshop on Article 88b of the Omnibus proposal and a potential standardization mandate for automated privacy signals. Discussion covered GPC adoption, the iOS/WebKit constraint on browser-level signals, and the policy boundary between targeting and attribution.
items.venue: RegTech4AI / Maastricht Law and Tech Lab, Brussels

rssMetadata.excerpt: Invited participation in the RegTech4AI expert workshop on Article 88b of the Omnibus proposal and a potential standardization mandate for automated privacy signals, covering GPC adoption and the policy boundary between targeting and attribution.
rssMetadata.venue: RegTech4AI / Maastricht Law and Tech Lab, Brussels

items.title: Roundtable: Anonymisation at the Crossroads of EU Digital Regulations
items.description: Expert participation in a public roundtable on DMA Article 6(11) organised by Sophie Stalla-Bourdillon at VUB Brussels Privacy Hub. Discussion covered anonymisation of search data, singling out as the central test, how sessionisation and linkability enable re-identification, and the risk of incoherence between DMA and GDPR anonymisation standards.

rssMetadata.excerpt: Expert participation in VUB Brussels Privacy Hub roundtable on DMA Article 6(11) and anonymisation, examining singling out as the central re-identification test and the risk of diverging standards between DMA and GDPR.

items.title: Bridging Borders with Trust: PETs to Preserve Customer Control in Cross-Border Legal Conflict
items.description: Panel at the Privacy Symposium 2026 in Venice examining how privacy-enhancing technologies, particularly confidential computing and trusted execution environments, can reconcile cross-border data flows with data residency requirements and regulatory friction across jurisdictions.

rssMetadata.excerpt: Panel at Privacy Symposium 2026 Venice on how confidential computing and trusted execution environments can reconcile cross-border data flows with data residency requirements and digital sovereignty frameworks.
